item.title: Why Pakistan is hosting the highest-level US-Iran talks in almost half a century
item.description: Officials from the US and Iran are set to meet in Islamabad, with Pakistan hoping to broker an end to the war in the Middle East.

item.title: Some Australians forced to choose 'between food and medicine', new survey finds
item.description: A new survey has shed light on the financial decisions faced by some Australians prescribed medicines not covered by the PBS.

item.title: Some Australians forced to choose 'between food and medicine', new survey finds
item.description: A new survey has shed light on the financial decisions faced by some Australians prescribed medicines not covered by the PBS.

item.title: 'Beaten' and 'subject to mock executions': How protesters are being forgotten in war
item.description: Several Iranians have been executed in recent weeks in Iran; some say the regime is sending the "most horrific warning signal".

item.title: Darwin multicultural walking group connecting migrants one step at a time
item.description: A local program in Darwin is helping refugees and new migrants connect with their community, while building life-long friendships. The walking group may be simple, but organisers say at a time when more people are facing conflict and displacement, the initiative provides a welcome escape.

item.title: More Australians are self-prescribing natural remedies, but are they always safe?
item.description: Widely used in cold and flu supplements, andrographis has been linked to life-threatening allergic reactions.

item.title: Government considering further economic relief as motorists urged to reduce fuel use
item.description: Transport Minister Catherine King has warned that even if the Strait of Hormuz opens soon, there's "a long tail" to the fuel crisis.

item.title: Better than Bolt: Gout Gout smashes 20-second barrier in 200m sprint, beats own record
item.description: The 18-year-old has become the first Australian to legally break the 20-second barrier over 200m after he recorded a time of 19.67.

item.title: 'Selfless, courageous' heroes of Bondi Junction stabbing honoured
item.description: Two years after a mass stabbing at a Sydney shopping centre, eight individuals have been recognised for their bravery.

item.title: What your dreams could be telling you about your Alzheimer's risk
item.description: Frequent nightmares, or the inability to recall your dreams at all, could be early signs of Alzheimer's disease.

item.title: Who is Péter Magyar, Hungary's new prime minister?
item.description: Railing against the Orbán regime helped his popularity explode. Now, he may be a problem for Trump and Putin.

item.title: Bondi stabbing heroes honoured with bravery awards | SBS News in Easy English 13 April 2026
item.description: A daily 5-minute news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

item.title: Blockading the Strait: How Donald Trump's latest strategy could cost Australians
item.description: The US leader has threatened to escalate the war on Iran with a naval blockade that experts warn could be economically disastrous.

item.title: Markets slip as oil surges and investors ask can AI interpret geopolitical risk?
item.description: The ASX edges lower as oil prices climb above US$100 amid escalating Middle East tensions, raising fresh concerns about inflation and growth. Armina Rosenberg from Minotaur Capital breaks down whether AI-driven investing can make sense of geopolitical shocks, while Deloitte Access Economics partner David Rumbens warns Australia could be edging closer to recession, even if the conflict eases. With markets still near record highs but volatility building, this episode unpacks the risks shaping the economic outlook and what it means for investors and households.

item.title: Viktor Orbán has been defeated. What does it mean for the rest of the world?
item.description: Viktor Orbán had such an outsized global influence that his crushing electoral defeat is resounding far and wide.

item.title: House prices are falling in some places, but one state keeps charging ahead
item.description: A property expert said houses in this capital city are selling at "crazy" prices.

item.title: Do young men 'want kids like kids want a puppy'? The findings on gen Z's 'cluck gap'
item.description: Research shows gen Z men are keener (or 'cluckier') on having children than women of the same generation. What's going on?

item.title: One Australian city is running out of landfill space, so its trash may head to these rural towns
item.description: Sydney's landfill capacity could run out by 2030, so the city's waste will be incinerated in regional towns — and locals aren't happy.

item.title: Coalition's migration plan panned as 'Trumpian', likened to 'White Australia' era
item.description: Labor, the Greens and rights groups have lined up to condemn Angus Taylor's first major policy proposal as Opposition leader.

item.title: What are Prince Harry and Meghan doing in Australia?
item.description: Despite bearing all the hallmarks of a royal tour, this is not an official visit.

item.title: Donald Trump refuses to apologise for strong criticism of Pope Leo | SBS News in Easy English 14 April 2026
item.description: A daily 5-minute news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

item.title: Man charged with using newly banned phrases in Queensland tries to argue law 'insane'
item.description: A 70-year-old activist has made a unique plea against charges of publicly reciting a prohibited expression during a protest.

item.title: From Jesus imagery to a 'holy war': Does Donald Trump really think he's divine?
item.description: There has been fierce backlash to Donald Trump's depiction of himself as a Jesus-like figure.

item.title: Qantas cancels flights as war hits corporate Australia's earnings
item.description: The ASX edges higher despite fresh warnings from corporate Australia, with Qantas and Westpac flagging the financial impact of the Middle East conflict as cost pressures build. Ricardo Gonçalves unpacks the latest market moves and what they mean for inflation and interest rates, speaking with NAB Chief Economist Sally Auld and LGT Wealth Management CIO Scott Haslem, while new data reveals rental stress has hit record levels. Cotality’s Head of Research Gerard Burg joins to explain why Australians are now spending more than ever on housing and what’s driving the shortage.

item.title: Strong backlash as Angus Taylor unveils Coalition’s controversial immigration policy
item.description: Opposition leader Angus Taylor has outlined the Coalition's new hardline immigration policy. The plan has drawn widespread criticism and strong condemnation from critics, including Labor and human rights groups.

item.title: Australians are the biggest cocaine users. But they don't know 'how many lives' are in a gram
item.description: The journey of cocaine from Colombia to Australia is stained with blood. But Colombia's president is trying to forge a new path.

item.title: Construction industry calls for 'once in a generation' reform ahead of housing crisis talks
item.description: Builders are calling for changes to the National Construction Code amid soaring overheads as regulatory costs bite.

item.title: What's at stake as Israel and Lebanon hold first talks in more than three decades
item.description: Lebanon and Israel have begun rare direct talks in Washington DC, but deep divisions and ongoing violence could stall any deal.

item.title: INTERVIEW: Singapore Energy Minister Dr Tan See Leng
item.description: After Prime Minister Albanese’s whistle-stop visit to Singapore, the future of Australia’s fuel supplies looks a lot more secure as the ripples from the Straits of Hormuz spread outwards. Last week Anthony Albanese met with his Singaporean counterpart Lawrence Wong in Singapore. The two signed off on a new bilateral trade agreement, under which Australia would guarantee Singapore’s supply of liquefied natural gas, and Singapore would prioritise the flow of refined petroleum to Australia. Singapore is Australia’s largest source of refined petrol. SBS's Naveen Razik secured an exclusive interview with Singapore's Energy Minister Dr. Tan See Leng about our energy partnership and the wider consequences of the current crisis in the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: Zelenskyy says US has 'no time for Ukraine' as Iran peace focus keeps negotiators busy
item.description: Zelenskyy pointed to the issue of deliveries of US arms to Ukraine, saying it had become "a big problem".

item.title: 'Really dangerous': Chalmers reacts to IMF's recession warning as he heads to US
item.description: The treasurer said Australia was "better placed" than many countries but would not be immune from the economic fallout.

item.title: 'Couldn't do it in Britain': Why Meghan and Harry's Australia tour is an 'experiment'
item.description: The four-day visit could be mistaken for a royal tour, but is actually a private trip considered more commercial in nature.

item.title: Damning ombudsman's report finds disabled brothers suffered after health department failures
item.description: The brothers, who were admitted to hospital and treated for severe malnutrition, are now in state care.

item.title: Speculation that US Iran peace talks could resume | SBS News in Easy English 15 April 2026
item.description: A daily 5-minute news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

item.title: Pastoral and political - the Pope visits Africa
item.description: Pope Leo the Fourteenth is on a historic tour of Africa, a journey spanning four countries, thousands of kilometres, and a continent that is now central to the future of the Catholic Church. From Algeria to Cameroon, Angola and Equatorial Guinea, the visit is being described as both pastoral and political.

item.title: How Trump's latest Iran war moves could bring China out of the shadows
item.description: Chinese President Xi Jinping made a rare public comment on the war in the Middle East.

item.title: 'Would be a different country': Burke defends migration as Taylor policy criticism mounts
item.description: The government has rejected the recently announced Coalition plans to deport migrants who breach "Australian values".

item.title: Airfares rise as Japan overtakes Indonesia as Australian favourite and ASX rises
item.description: Airfares are set to climb as Virgin Australia cuts flights to manage rising fuel costs, while Qantas flags a sharp increase in its fuel bill, reshaping travel plans for Australians. Phoebe Deas speaks with Ellis Taylor from Cirium on what these changes mean for the aviation sector, as new data reveals Japan has overtaken Indonesia as Australia’s second most popular destination, helped by a stronger exchange rate. Meanwhile, the ASX closes slightly higher, with insight from Scott Phillips of The Motley Fool on the market’s mixed performance.

item.title: INTERVIEW: Vanuatu's minister for Climate Change Ralph Regenvanu
item.description: Vanuatu’s Minister for Climate Change, Ralph Regenvanu, has emerged as one of the Pacific’s most influential and principled public figures, combining political leadership with cultural advocacy and a sustained commitment to climate justice. In an exclusive interview with SBS, he said that current issues with fuel security and supply wouldn’t influence the negotiations between Australia and Vanuatu to finalise the Nakamal agreement. The treaty, initialed in August last year but not finalised, aims to enhance economic, security and cultural cooperation between the two countries. He told SBS’s Rayane Tamer that the current fuel crisis presents Australia with a unique opportunity to become an alternative energy superpower

item.title: The groceries that could rise 20 per cent in price, as farmers issue warning
item.description: Australian farmers are warning Australians will be hit by food price increases due to ongoing diesel and fertiliser supply constraints.

item.title: The lessons from the Middle East war Australia can't afford to ignore
item.description: Global conflict is exposing Australia's vulnerabilities in energy, trade and defence — testing the nation's preparedness.

item.title: 'This is a setback': Bowen outlines impact of oil refinery blaze as investigation begins
item.description: A Viva Energy spokesperson said the company would conduct a "thorough investigation" into the incident.

item.title: 'This is a setback': Bowen outlines impact of oil refinery blaze as investigation begins
item.description: A Viva Energy spokesperson said the company would conduct a "thorough investigation" into the incident.

item.title: What we know so far about the oil refinery fire in Geelong
item.description: An investigation is underway to determine the cause of the fire, but it is not being as suspicious. Here's what we know so far.

item.title: US pilot Daniel Duggan set to be extradited from Australia after failed appeal
item.description: A federal court judge has dismissed former US Marine pilot Daniel Duggan's extradition appeal.

item.title: Israeli strikes kill four medics, targeting two rescue teams, Lebanese authorities say
item.description: Lebanese authorities accused the Israeli military of deliberately targeting rescue workers.

item.title: Fire at fuel refinery in Geelong | | SBS News in Easy English 16 April 2026
item.description: A daily 5-minute news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

item.title: Import nation: How Australia went from eight oil refineries to two
item.description: Australia only has two oil refineries and one caught fire overnight, igniting further concerns about the nation's fuel security.

item.title: Minns says NSW government 'disappointed' as protest laws ruled unconstitutional
item.description: A judge in NSW's top court has struck down the state's laws regarding restrictions on protests.

item.title: How Australia's defence spend compares globally, as Marles' numbers questioned
item.description: The government's newly announced 2026 National Defence Strategy will see the budget grow by $53 billion over the next decade.

item.title: Trump’s Hormuz gambit, Hungary ousts Orbán & Colombia’s cocaine war
item.description: US-Iran talks collapse, and China feels the pressure over President Trump’s blockade in the Strait of Hormuz. Plus, a stunning election victory signals a new era for Hungary and how Australia is helping to fuel Colombia’s drug trade.

item.title: ASX falls despite Wall Street record as jobless rate holds and AI reshapes workplace
item.description: The ASX eases despite strong leads from Wall Street, while Australia’s unemployment rate holds at 4.3 per cent and companies grapple with rising costs. Ricardo Gonçalves speaks with HSBC Chief Economist Paul Bloxham on what the latest labour market data means for interest rates, and with David Lane from Ord Minnett on the day’s market moves. Plus, Mark Thirlwell from the Australian Institute of Company Directors explains why artificial intelligence and productivity pressures are keeping business leaders awake at night.

item.title: Creative accounting or record investments? Labor unveils 2026 National Defence Strategy
item.description: The Australian Government has unveiled the 2026 National Defence Strategy, outlining reprioritised investments in drones, missile defence and undersea warfare capabilities. While the government claims the new strategy includes record defence spending allocations, the opposition has accused Labor of artificially inflating the numbers.

item.title: How to get the best view of the Lyrid meteor shower in Australian skies
item.description: Stargazers can rejoice as another celestial event passes through Earth's atmosphere.

item.title: At least 16 in Ukraine reportedly killed as Russia launches 2026's deadliest attack so far
item.description: Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy said the attack proved that Russia didn't deserve any easing of sanctions.

item.title: Australia's neighbour criticises Albanese and will 'definitely' ask China for fuel help
item.description: Vanuatu has urged Australia to confront its reliance on fossil fuels, while not ruling out seeking support from Canberra.

item.title: The 'crop' we're not harvesting: Why Australia's brightest source is still underused
item.description: As solar farms spread across regional Australia, some farmers are cashing in while others say the rollout is too fast.

item.title: 'Uncharted territory': How the war in the Middle East is testing the FIFA World Cup
item.description: Iran's participation in the World Cup is looking uncertain, posing a complex logistical and geopolitical challenge for FIFA.

item.title: Pope Leo slams 'tyrants' as Trump says the pontiff must understand 'nasty world'
item.description: US President Donald Trump says it's important for Pope Leo to understand Iran is a global threat, having called him "weak" on foreign policy.

item.title: Calls to drop demonstrators' charges as NSW anti-protest laws ruled unconstitutional
item.description: The court's decision could prompt prosecutors to withdraw charges and pave the way for others to sue.

item.title: Iranian footballers thank Australia for 'safe haven' but ask for privacy
item.description: Two Iranian players have expressed their gratitude for being offered a safe haven in Australia after the Asian Cup tournament.

item.title: Ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon | SBS News in Easy English 17 April 2026
item.description: A daily 5-minute news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

item.title: Fakes, missile cruisers, unusual encounters: A front-row seat to the global energy crisis
item.description: The aftershocks of the closure of the Strait of Hormuz can be seen with the naked eye at one of the world's busiest ports.

item.title: Struggling to find a job, Victor makes a living by crying for strangers
item.description: Mourners for hire can help families offer a "better send-off" in communities where funerals are steeped in culture.

item.title: Paid to Cry: Kenya’s Professional Mourners in Demand
item.description: How do you guarantee a good turn out at a funeral? After all, the last thing you want to happen as you pass on to whatever lies beyond the grave... is to go without at least a bit of fuss. In Kenya, some families are hiring professional mourners to give their loved ones the ultimate send-off.

item.title: ASX ends three week winning streak despite another record on Wall Street
item.description: The ASX200 has edged lower, snapping a three-week run despite fresh record highs on Wall Street, with weakness in consumer discretionary stocks offset by gains in tech and buy now, pay later players. In this short end-of-week update, Ricardo Gonçalves speaks with Dianne Colledge from Morgans Financial Limited about what is driving the local market, the outlook for key sectors and how global momentum is shaping sentiment at home, alongside moves in the Australian dollar and oil prices.

item.title: 'Borrowing from the past': This old way of living is making a comeback in Australia
item.description: My grandparents' home is my home too, and that's becoming more common in Australia.

item.title: With more rate hikes looming, here's what borrowers should do
item.description: As economists warn of more interest rate hikes by Christmas, some borrowers are wondering if it's time to refinance their mortgage.

item.title: Iran threatens to re-close Strait of Hormuz if US doesn't withdraw blockade
item.description: United States President Donald Trump has said the US' blockade would remain until "our transaction with Iran is 100% complete".

item.title: Iran threatens to re-close Strait of Hormuz if US doesn't withdraw blockade
item.description: United States President Donald Trump has said the US' blockade would remain until "our transaction with Iran is 100% complete".

item.title: Simmering tensions: Tripe soup row boils over
item.description: A beloved dish shared by both Turkish and Greek people has sparked new cultural tensions between the two countries.A Greek restaurant owner is striving to register tripe soup with the United Nations' cultural body, UNESCO, but the move has sparked uproar in neighbouring Turkiye.

item.title: Albanese welcomes reopening of Strait of Hormuz but says arrangement still 'very fragile'
item.description: The prime minister also said that "Australia stands ready to support efforts to restore stability and security" in the strait.

item.title: 'It impacted the relationship with my culture': When culture, cuisine and a chronic illness combine
item.description: Coeliac disease affects one in seventy Australians, according to the Wesley Research Institute. But when food is integral to cultural identity, the impacts of this autoimmune disease — especially on multicultural and diasporic communities in Australia — can extend beyond the physical, affecting mental health. Jude Soussan is a young person of Lebanese heritage in Perth, Western Australia, and has used her diagnosis to help educate others about what it means to live with coeliac disease in the diaspora, using creativity. She hopes that, with time, communities can better understand how to accommodate chronic illness.

item.title: The 'real tightrope' Australia will have to walk to ease inflation and cut oil dependence
item.description: Experts say the fuel crunch and war in the Middle East present an opportunity to reduce Australia's dependence on imported fuels.

item.title: What Gallipoli can teach us about today's war in the Middle East
item.description: The war in the Middle East reminds how easily small strategic moves can mushroom into expanding military commitments.

item.title: While the world's eyes were on the moon, Australia quietly axed a major space deal
item.description: The end of a significant membership has some wondering whether Australia's days as an astronomy powerhouse are numbered.

item.title: Twenty protesters arrested for violating Queensland's ban on pro-Palestinian phrases
item.description: Those who recite or display "from the river to the sea" or "globalise the intifada" in Queensland can face up to two years' jail.

item.title: 'A stable life': The matchmaker setting up North Korean women with South Korean men
item.description: Yujin runs a matchmaking agency — and her clients are North Korean women and South Korean men looking to date.

item.title: 'Damn it, let's go after him': What the Democrats need to topple Donald Trump
item.description: The midterm elections are on the horizon, and the contest to succeed Donald Trump is quietly underway.

item.title: All change for the FIFA World Cup 2026
item.description: In just a few weeks, the world’s football fans will be turning all their attention on North America, as the highly anticipated FIFA World Cup finally gets underway. But some may find it’s not entirely what they were expecting.

item.title: 'Situation is dire': No progress amid 35th anniversary of deaths in custody report
item.description: Since the report's release, more than 630 Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people have died in the custody of police or corrective services.

item.title: Australians cut back on stockpiling petrol, reduce fuel spend as households adjust
item.description: New analysis from a major bank shows Australians have cut their spending on fuel and are going back to filling up when they need it, rather than panic buying.

item.title: The simple medication switch that could save Australians over $165 million
item.description: Among other changes, a basic software update could save many Australian patients both time and money.

item.title: Between two homes: Migrant workers in Lebanon roped into social and economic impact of war
item.description: Migrant workers in Lebanon are one of the most vulnerable communities since the war in the Middle East erupted. Experts say they are trapped between the economic impacts of the war, and it's taking a toll on their sense of belonging.

item.title: Ben Roberts-Smith vows to fight all charges, criticises police over 'sensational arrest'
item.description: Ben Roberts-Smith says he is proud of his service in Afghanistan and will fight the charges against him.

item.title: 'One thing after another': The almost $20,000 penalty for being single in Australia
item.description: Being single comes at a "premium" in Australia, leaving those not in a relationship thousands of dollars worse off.

item.title: Over 60 per cent of popular Cheaper Home Batteries Program installations 'substandard'
item.description: A new regulatory report says the installation problems it found risk not only the credibility of the scheme, but also public safety.

item.title: 'We haven't learned anything': A looming 'crunch' for Australia's food system
item.description: Australia's approach to fertiliser import disruptions means doubling down on a fragile, fracturing system, some experts say.

item.title: Eight children killed in Louisiana domestic violence related shooting, police say
item.description: Some of the children were related to the suspected gunman.

item.title: Cash was meant to be dying. But new RBA data tells a different story
item.description: Cash use ticked up for the first time since 2007 — and one in three Australians say they'd struggle without it, according to a survey.

item.title: Illicit tobacco remains widespread across Australia
Interest-free loans announced for Australian businesses | SBS News in Easy English 20 April 2026
item.description: A daily 5-minute news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

item.title: Erol has long waited for rules to change. Today, he finally gave whole blood
item.description: "This is a very meaningful time for us to feel viscerally that we are more included within society," Erol Dalkic said.

item.title: Fire-hit refinery set to return to near full capacity in weeks, owner says
item.description: The Geelong oil refinery was ravaged by a blaze last week, sparking further supply fears amid a global fuel crisis.

item.title: Pope Leo is celebrated as he visits Angola
item.description: Pope Leo’s Africa tour is drawing large turnouts and warm welcomes. In Angola, the mood is celebratory, while in Washington D-C, not so much, as Pope Leo addresses the US President's criticism of him.

item.title: Free RSV vaccinations for older Australians
item.description: The Federal Government has announced free R-S-V vaccinations for older Australians, in a push to better protect those most at risk from the potentially deadly respiratory virus. The $445 million dollar investment means Australians aged 75 and over - and Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people aged 60 and over - will be able to access the immunisation for free from mid May, saving them as much as $300 dollars per vaccination.

item.title: Mass shooting triggers calls for more action on domestic violence in the US
item.description: There are calls for more resources to combat domestic violence in the United States, after the deadliest mass shooting in that country in more than two years. The gunman shot dead eight children in the southern US state of Louisiana - before he was fatally shot by police.

item.title: United States fires at, seizes Iranian-flagged cargo ship
item.description: The United States has seized an Iranian-flagged cargo ship it says tried to pass its naval blockade in the northern Arabian sea, near the Strait of Hormuz. It comes as Pakistan prepares to host another round of talks between the U-S and Iran, but it's unclear if the talks will go ahead.

item.title: A country under 'strain': Australians' life satisfaction hits record low
item.description: New data shows life satisfaction has hit a record low, dipping below levels seen during COVID-19 lockdowns.

item.title: $2 petrol prices likely here to stay, as government looks to new instant tax deduction
item.description: Energy stocks lag, as oil prices remained volatile around supply concerns in the Strait of Hormuz, even as petrol prices eased, though still well above last year’s levels. Market analyst Evan Lucas unpacks what it all means for households and investors, as attention also turns to the federal government’s new $1,000 instant tax deduction set to benefit more than six million workers. Mark Chapman from H&R Block explains who gains from that, how it works, and when the savings will flow through.

item.title: Tsunami warning issued after powerful earthquake strikes off northern Japan coast
item.description: Buildings as far away as Tokyo shook during the tremor, and authorities warned further quakes could strike in coming days.

item.title: New details reveal possible 'urgent' recommendations in antisemitism interim report
item.description: The first public hearings from the royal commission into antisemitism will be held days after an interim report from the inquiry is due.

item.title: Inside the 'military operation' of one of Australia's rarest parenting experiences
item.description: Parents of triplets face heavy financial and emotional pressures. Are Australia's support systems built for families like theirs?

item.title: Already under pressure, Australia’s food system could now be in big trouble
item.description: Australia should be a rich global food bowl, but the future is looking more uncertain — for a range of reasons.

item.title: Woolworths hits back at allegations it misled customers with 'prices dropped' campaign
item.description: The consumer watchdog is alleging the supermarket giant used "illusory discounts" to sell Tim Tams and 11 other popular items.

item.title: Tim Cook steps down as Apple CEO, naming John Ternus as replacement
item.description: Apple has chosen the leader who will steer it through the AI era as Tim Cook wraps up 15 years at the helm.

item.title: Tim Cook steps down as Apple CEO, naming John Ternus as replacement
item.description: Apple has chosen the leader who will steer it through the AI era as Tim Cook wraps up 15 years at the helm.

item.title: Wong's flurry of talks as Albanese warns Australians will feel 'long tail' of war
item.description: Rising global instability threatens fuel supplies and trade, with leaders warning of lasting economic impacts in Australia.

item.title: NASA rolls out core stage for the next mission to the moon: Artemis III
item.description: It's been a week since the crew of Artemis II (2) dropped back to earth after a record-breaking mission, but concrete steps are already being taken to achieve the next phase of NASA's lunar goals. The Michoud Assembly Facility in New Orleans has rolled out the largest section of NASA's Space Launch System rocket, which will launch a second crewed mission known as Artemis III (3).

item.title: Iran and the US offer mixed messages on path ahead with end of ceasefire in sight
item.description: It's still unclear whether Iran will come to the table for talks with the United States on Tuesday, just as a two-week ceasefire is set to expire. Tehran continues to resist pressure from the United States and Israel to agree to their nuclear deal, while tensions escalate over ensuring safe passageway through critical global shipping route the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: SBS News in Easy English 21 April 2026
item.description: A daily five minute news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

item.title: Explained: The Peter Mandelson saga and the crisis facing UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer
item.description: Keir Starmer was praised for appointing Peter Mandelson as ambassador to the US — a decision that's put his political career at risk.

item.title: A $71bn rebuild and 77-year setback: A 'catastrophic' crisis in the Middle East
item.description: The cost of rebuilding Gaza and the "catastrophic" human toll of war have been laid bare in a new report.

item.title: Starmer again fends off calls for his resignation over Mandelson appointment
item.description: UK Prime Minister Sir Keir Starmer is again rejecting calls for his resignation over the appointment of Peter Mandelson as Ambassador to the US .He has shifted blame to foreign ministry officials , who he says withheld vetting concerns about Mr Mandelson that would have halted his employment.

item.title: SBS World News talks to Assistant Minister for Citizenship Julian Hill
item.description: SBS federal political reporters Anna Henderson, Rayane Tamer, and Naveen Razik have an in-depth conversation with Assistant Minister for Citizenship, Customs, and Multicultural Affairs, Julian Hill, about visa categories, and the task the government faces in dealing with humanitarian visas and family reunion.

item.title: Markets pause, as Apple turns the page on Tim Cook's era
item.description: The ASX 200 edged lower as investors held back ahead of a key Middle East ceasefire deadline, with Michael Wayne from Medallion Financial Group unpacking the day’s market moves. At the same time, Apple enters a new chapter as Tim Cook steps down after 15 years, having overseen explosive growth and the launch of products like the Apple Watch and AirPods. Stephanie Youssef speaks with Wedbush Securities’ Dan Ives about Cook’s legacy, the challenges facing incoming CEO John Ternus, and why artificial intelligence could define Apple’s next phase.

item.title: Pressure builds to lift Australia's 'tiny' gas tax
item.description: As calls mount for a 25 per cent tax on gas exports, a leading economist has trashed the existing measures at a Senate inquiry.

item.title: The US-Iran ceasefire has been extended. But were the warring states ever in agreement?
item.description: As a two-week ceasefire appears to be extended, experts ask whether there was any agreement in the first place.

item.title: I'm the only triplet in my family with cerebral palsy. Being a 'criplet' led to a comedy career
item.description: Growing up as a triplet, Alexandra Hudson didn't miss out on anything. She says this started to change while recovering from surgery at 15.

item.title: The 'horrific' truth about the romance scammers targeting vulnerable Australians
item.description: Behind the everyday scams targeting Australians, there are scores of vulnerable victims trafficked into the job.

item.title: The 'horrific' truth about the romance scammers targeting vulnerable Australians
item.description: Behind the everyday scams targeting Australians, there are scores of vulnerable victims trafficked into the job.

item.title: Trump extends ceasefire deadline | Morning News Bulletin 22 April 2026
item.description: Ceasefire deadline extended as the world waits to see whether Iran will join peace talks; NDIS cuts due to be outlined today; the Sydney Swans prepare for a tough test against the Western Bulldogs.

item.title: SBS World News talks to Immigration Minister Tony Burke
item.description: SBS News political reporter Anna Henderson speaks to Immigration Minister Tony Burke about visas, the War in the Middle East, and IS brides. Mr Burke says federal Opposition Leader Angus Taylor is trying to turn Australians against one another with his policies.

item.title: Trump extends ceasefire with Iran to encourage negotiations, says US military is 'raring to go'
item.description: Iran said on Tuesday it had yet to decide whether to attend last-ditch peace talks with the US.

item.title: Hezbollah fires at Israel, citing truce breaches as ceasefire negotiations near
item.description: The US will mediate another round of negotiations between the Israeli and Lebanese governments later this week.

item.title: Pakistan welcomes Iran ceasefire extension | Midday News Bulletin 22 April 2026
item.description: Pakistani mediators welcome ceasefire extension ; fourth man charged over kidnapping and murder of a Sydney grandfather; Formula One shifting back toward hybrid and combustion power

item.title: The US-Iran ceasefire has been extended. But were the warring states ever in agreement?
item.description: The US-Iran ceasefire has been extended, just as it was due to expire. US President Donald Trump says it will continue so Iran can submit their proposal for a ceasefire and talks can take place. But the US plans to continue its blockade of Iranian ports, an move that Iranian officials have maintained is an act of war and a violation of the ceasefire agreement. It's led to confusion over whether the ceasefire talks will happen.

item.title: 'We're not ready': Calls for AI regulation gain momentum
item.description: As new autonomous AI technology can now be accessed by everyday users, the calls for AI regulation are getting louder.

item.title: Trump remains defiant on US blockade of Iranian ports | SBS News in Easy English 22 April 2026
item.description: A daily 5-minute news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

item.title: 'We're the mugs here': Is Japan getting Australian gas for free?
item.description: Discussions on Australia's gas and oil resources are heating up as a 25 per cent gas export tax gains traction.

item.title: With a degenerative disability, Jane is unsure of her future. Now she's fearful of NDIS cuts
item.description: Disability advocates have flagged concerns that an overhaul of the NDIS scheme could disproportionately impact parts of the community.

item.title: Government announces major cuts to NDIS | Evening News Bulletin 22 April 2026
item.description: The federal government tightens access to the NDIS; Military planners meeting in London on how to re-open the Strait of Hormuz; Essendon's Jordan Ridley sidelined with a calf strain days before an iconic Anzac Day match.

item.title: 'Interconnected challenges': Why Lebanon's future could determine the course of war
item.description: Israel and Lebanon agreed to implement a "cessation of hostilities" for 10 days, but time is running out for a peace deal.

item.title: Woolworths executive says 'prices dropped' rules changed as inflation grew
item.description: The ACCC claims the discounted prices were often the same or higher than the original shelf prices.

item.title: "Difficult, unavoidable": NDIS overhaul aims to reduce participation and target fraudulent providers
item.description: The federal government has announced a major overhaul of the National Disability Insurance Scheme in efforts to cut down fraud and spending in the $50 billion program. Welcoming some aspects of the reform, disability advocates say there must be further clarity around cuts to support and eligibility, with fears that those in need will lose access to vital supports.

item.title: Super funds recover March fall as middle east war impacts company earnings
item.description: The Australian sharemarket slipped 1.1 per cent, yet remains within reach of a record high, continuing its recovery from March’s downturn as super funds rebound and long-term gains hold steady. In this episode of SBS On the Money, Ricardo Gonçalves unpacks the day’s biggest moves, including Cochlear’s dramatic 40 per cent fall to a 10-year low after a profit warning tied to weak US consumer confidence and global uncertainty. Portfolio manager James Gerrish from Market Partners joins the conversation to discuss whether other companies will also be hit by the war in the Middle-East.

item.title: NASA hopes its new telescope will help it chart an 'atlas of the universe'
item.description: Roman is expected to discover tens of thousands of planets beyond our solar system.

item.title: Iranian gunboat fires at container ship off Oman, ship off Iran also targeted
item.description: The vessel had been sailing under a Liberian flag and "had been informed it had permission to transit the Strait of Hormuz."

item.title: Concerned about footing the bill for NDIS changes, states are pushing back
item.description: In address to the National Press Club on Wednesday, Mark Butler said states would have "greater funding responsibility" for the scheme.

item.title: Online watchdog puts video game giants on notice as child grooming concerns rise
item.description: The news comes after Roblox recently introduced new account types for underage users as pressure from global regulators mounted.

item.title: ‘That future was gone’: The pain of losing your life partner young
item.description: The death of a partner can feel debilitating at any age, but in your 20s and 30s, it brings unique challenges.

item.title: 'Can no longer be stretched': The 'defining shift' that has renters at their limits
item.description: Sky-high rents persist, but price growth is slowing and diverging across Australia's capitals, according to a report.

item.title: 'Ultimate breeding machines': The rodent boom is back — and Aussie farmers are scrambling
item.description: The CSIRO has warned that mouse populations have exploded, but the cause of the outbreak remains a partial mystery.

item.title: Iran seizes ships crossing Hormuz | Morning News Bulletin 23 April 2026
item.description: In this bulletin, Iran says it has seized cargo ships near the Strait of Hormuz, the Coalition says there's been little consultation on changes to the NDIS. And in NRL, St George Illawarra’s Kade Reed says he's looking forward to his Anzac Day debut.

item.title: Iran seizes two ships crossing Hormuz, says it won't reopen strait while US blockade remains
item.description: Iran's Revolutionary Guards said their naval forces had seized two container ships trying to cross the strategic strait.

item.title: Lebanese journalist Amal Khalil killed by Israeli strike amid ceasefire
item.description: Rescuers said they had recovered the body of Amal Khalil after she was killed in an Israeli airstrike.

item.title: INTERVIEW: Why have the ceasefire talks stalled?
item.description: Ceasefire talks between the US and Iran seem to be on hold. Pakistan had planned to host a second round of talks, but the White House suspended Vice President JD Vance’s planned trip to Islamabad - as Iran rebuffed efforts to restart negotiations. Pakistani officials say authorities will keep security arrangements in place in Islamabad in case US and Iranian delegations ultimately arrive. Speaking to the Associated Press, Qamar Cheema, the Executive Director of the Sanober Institute in Islamabad, says talks between the U.S. and Iran have stalled due to a lack of clarity and a complex power struggle within Tehran

item.title: Who gets a public holiday for Anzac Day across Australia?
item.description: Anzac Day falls on a Saturday this year, with new rule changes coming into effect for the first time.

item.title: Israel accused of war crimes after Lebanese journalist killed | Midday News Bulletin 23 April 2026
item.description: In this bulletin, a Lebanese journalist is killed in Israeli strikes on southern Israel, National Cabinet meets to discuss a fuel strategy. And in golf, Australian champion Hannah Green shares her goal for the Chevron Championship.

item.title: Iran seizes ships in Strait of Hormuz in escalation of naval blockade
item.description: Iran has seized two ships crossing the Strait of Hormuz. It says it won't reopen the crucial waterway while a US blockade of its vessels and ports remains in place.

item.title: Ship captain talks of distress after 54 days stranded in Persian Gulf | SBS News in Easy English 23 April 2026
item.description: A daily 5-minute news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

item.title: James Valentine remembered as a voice of warmth and wit after his death at 64
item.description: Radio and music personality James Valentine has died two years after being diagnosed with cancer.

item.title: Who holds the power? The key question behind Trump's latest Iran demand
item.description: The US president wants a "unified" response — but some argue there could be "division" among Iranian officials on a potential peace deal.

item.title: Albanese defends decision to move 160,000 off NDIS | Evening News Bulletin 23 April 2026
item.description: Anthony Albanese defends the government's changes to the NDIS; Australia's petrol reserves climb to 46 days; Carlton coach Michael Voss says there's been amazing care and love shown for Elijah Hollands.

item.title: TACO Trump? An idiot’s guide to Strait talking & Marcos Jnr gym junkie
item.description: Confusion deepens on the Strait of Hormuz as President Trump extends the ceasefire with Iran. Another truce hangs in the balance with Israel and Hezbollah trading accusations - while thousands of Israeli troops draw a line in southern Lebanon. Plus, Japan loosens its long-standing arms export restrictions and world leaders flex their fitness credentials.

item.title: Advocates cheer decision to make showers free for home-based aged care
item.description: The lion's share of attention this week has been on the National Disability Insurance Scheme, with the federal government announcing cuts that it's estimated will see tens of thousands removed from the scheme. But there have also been developments in aged care too- the federal government has now reversed its policy on the funding for showering after a mass of complaints from residents, families, and advocates.

item.title: The burning questions at the centre of the NDIS reset
item.description: All participants under the scheme will be reassessed under a new method — causing anxiety around losing funding.

item.title: Employers seeking more workers with AI skills, ASX pulls further away from record
item.description: The ASX slipped 0.6 per cent amid rising geopolitical tensions in the Gulf, with losses led by materials and the big banks, while energy stocks climbed on a surge in oil prices. Against that backdrop, a sharp shift in the jobs market is emerging, with SEEK reporting a 75 per cent rise in advertised roles mentioning AI skills.

item.title: Australia now has more fuel on hand than before the war in the Middle East began
item.description: Despite the strong fuel stores, forecasts suggest Australia's economy could take a big hit if disruptions to the Strait of Hormuz continue.

item.title: Ben Roberts-Smith was planning to leave Australia, court documents show
item.description: Documents from the 47-year-old's bail hearing released Thursday detail plans to open a business overseas.

item.title: Former Philippines president Duterte to face ICC trial on crimes against humanity
item.description: Judges said there were "substantial grounds" to believe Duterte played a key role in the murders of 76 people.

item.title: Pentagon estimates suggest clearing Strait of Hormuz mines could take six months
item.description: The Pentagon's assessment added that it was unlikely any mine clearing operations would begin before the end of the war.

item.title: Australia's Twelve Apostles draw millions. Now, their mysteries have been solved
item.description: The enigma of Australia's iconic limestone stacks has been uncovered.

item.title: 'Highly vulnerable': Could Queensland's controversial hate speech laws be quashed in court?
item.description: The controversial new laws targeting antisemitism have seen dozens of Queenslanders arrested — including some from the Jewish community.

item.title: The Tucker Carlson rift: Has Trump lost one of his most influential allies for good?
item.description: The former Fox News host went scorched earth this week, admitting he would be "tormented" by his past support for Trump.

item.title: Trump orders US military to 'shoot and kill' Iranian boats laying mines | Morning News Bulletin 24 April 2026
item.description: Donald Trump orders the US military to 'shoot and kill' Iranian boats laying mines in the Strait of Hormuz; Fears remote Indigenous communities could be left without any power; Jamarra Ugle-Hagan to make his long-awaited debut with the Gold Coast Suns.

item.title: Israel-Lebanon confirm three-week ceasefire extension after White House negotiations
item.description: Thursday marked the most significant test of the ceasefire yet, after Israeli strikes killed a Lebanese journalist.

item.title: UK to ban everyone born after 2008 from buying cigarettes
item.description: The British health secretary says the move will create a "smoke-free" generation.

item.title: INTERVIEW: Will things ever return to 'normal' in the Strait of Hormuz?
item.description: Shipping traffic through the Strait of Hormuz remains at a virtual standstill with President Trump ordering US forces to 'shoot and kill' Iranian vessels laying mines in the Strait. The US military says it seized another tanker associated with smuggling Iranian oil in the Indian Ocean. The U.S. and Iran had declared the Strait open on Friday April 17 but Iran reimposed control on Saturday. Tehran said it was responding to a continued U.S. blockade of Iranian ports, calling it a violation of their ceasefire. So will we ever see a return to normal shipping and trade, once the strait reopens? In this episode of Weekend One on One, we hear from Alexis Ellender, senior lead analyst at maritime data firm Kpler

item.title: For Gen Z, dumb phones and CDs are the winners as they return to analog
item.description: Analog devices are making a comeback with the most tech-savvy, plugged in generation of all time - Gen Z. Why are Gen Z using tech from a time they don't remember?

item.title: Lebanon and Israel agree to extend three-week ceasefire | Midday News Bulletin 24 April 2026
item.description: A ceasefire between Lebanon and Israel extended by three weeks; First Nations Australians concerned over NDIS reform; Reaction to a proposal for Italy to replace Iran at the World Cup.

item.title: Alarm as Iran's 'sacrificing life' war campaign in Australia lets children sign up
item.description: The Australian Federal Police is aware of the campaign that has been promoted by the Iranian embassy in Canberra.

item.title: Israel–Lebanon ceasefire extended | SBS News in Easy English 24 April 2026
item.description: A daily 5-minute news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

item.title: A Trump envoy said Italy should replace Iran at the World Cup. Then things kicked off
item.description: Iran and Italy have reacted strongly to suggestions that Italy could be substituted for Iran at the World Cup.

item.title: A Trump envoy said Italy should replace Iran at the World Cup. Then things kicked off
item.description: Iran and Italy have reacted strongly to suggestions that Italy could be substituted for Iran at the World Cup.

item.title: Trump won’t be rushed to end Middle East war | Evening News Bulletin 24 April 2026
item.description: US President Donald Trump says he won't be rushed to end the war in the Middle East; the government says building new refineries not a solution to current fuel pressures; Penrith prepare to take on the Knights in Newcastle.

item.title: India rebukes Donald Trump for sharing remarks calling it a 'hellhole' on social media
item.description: The comments, shared by Trump on social media, came from a conservative commentator on a talk radio show.

item.title: Attacks continue in southern Lebanon despite three-week ceasefire extension
item.description: Donald Trump has announced a three-week extension to the ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon during a press conference at the White House. He says he hopes more leadership-level talks will take place over coming weeks, as he seeks to bring the broader conflict with Iran to a close.

item.title: Markets slip as tech earnings loom and trust in finfluencers questioned
item.description: The ASX200 edged lower by 0.1 per cent, capping a weekly fall of 1.8 per cent, as global markets pulled back from record highs and investors turned their attention to upcoming tech earnings, with Tony Sycamore from IG Markets unpacking what’s ahead. Meanwhile, new data from ASIC reveals a growing number of young Australians are turning to AI platforms, social media and so-called finfluencers for financial information, raising concerns about credibility and oversight. ASIC Commissioner Alan Kirkland explains the regulator’s response.

item.title: The women facing 'impossible choices' as a crisis grips Australia
item.description: Cost of living pressures are thought to be exacerbating instances of domestic and family violence.

item.title: 'A real risk': Financial watchdog's warning about finfluencers
item.description: Four finfluencers have been issued warning notices for allegedly providing unlicensed financial advice.

item.title: Government says 'no change' in position amid growing calls for 25 per cent gas export tax
item.description: Independent senator Allegra Spender said a 25 per cent tax on gas exports would help fix issues in the sector.

item.title: One of the world's rarest instruments, stolen by Nazis, may have reappeared in France
item.description: An expert on looted musical instruments is on the trail of a lost violin by Italian luthier Antonio Stradivari dating back to 1719.

item.title: Australia marks Anzac Day in Dawn Services across the country | Morning News Bulletin 25 April 2026
item.description: Australia marks Anzac Day in Dawn Services across the country; the White House says US envoys will travel to Pakistan to meet with Iran's foreign minister; Mariners forward Ali Auglah hoping for a World Cup call up - for Iraq.

item.title: Amid war crimes investigations, some veterans are grappling with the Anzac legend
item.description: As Anzac commemorations are held across the country, many are reflecting on the legacy of service in the wake of alleged war crimes.

item.title: Fuel prices under threat, jobs transforming — and a new tax break for Australians
item.description: The key money stories this week, broken down simply and what they mean for you.

item.title: ‘Tea with heart’ – meet the military veteran giving back to others who serve
item.description: A chain of specialty tea bars founded by a military veteran is expanding rapidly. Ambi's Chai serves tea blended with exotic spices - and its founder is proud his cafes are bringing communities closer.

item.title: Valentine went to war for Australia. It took his family 100 years to find him
item.description: Hundreds of Indigenous Australians volunteered to serve in World War One despite being restricted from serving.

item.title: Fighting for a country that denied their rights: Remembering the service of Australia's Indigenous veterans
item.description: [[WARNING: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people should exercise caution when listening to this audio as it contains names and images of deceased persons, which may cause distress to members of these communities.]] Since the First World War, Indigenous men and women have served in Australia's defence forces, despite the government's refusal to recognise their rights. NITV Radio's Kerri-Lee Barry has spoken to Michael Bell about members of her own family who served in Australia's defence forces.

item.title: Australians mark Anzac Day at Gallipoli, across the Middle East and at home
item.description: Thousands have gathered at dawn services on the Gallipoli peninsla and around the country to honour Australians who served.

item.title: Meet the military veteran creating comfort and connection through chai
item.description: A veteran brews more than chai. He's stirring connection, culture and community one sip at a time.

item.title: Booing during Anzac Day commemorations condemned | Midday News Bulletin 25 April 2026
item.description: Booing during Anzac Day commemorations condemned; Australian families believed to be linked to the IS group leave a refugee camp in Syria; Socceroos defender Harry Souttar makes a victorious comeback.

item.title: Beaconsfield 20 years on: the Australian spirit of grit and humour remembered
item.description: The two-week rescue of Beaconsfield miners Todd Russell and Brant Webb remembered twenty years on.

item.title: Indigenous leaders, politicians and military chiefs condemn Anzac Day booing
item.description: Booing disrupted Welcome to Country addresses at Anzac Day dawn services in Melbourne, Perth and Sydney where a man was arrested.

item.title: Indigenous leaders, politicians and military chiefs condemn Anzac Day booing
item.description: Booing disrupted Welcome to Country addresses at Anzac Day dawn services in Melbourne, Perth and Sydney where a man was arrested.

item.title: Anzac Day dawn services marred by booing | Evening News Bulletin 25 April 2026
item.description: Anzac Day dawn services marred by booing; The federal government says it's not playing any role in helping to repatriate the Australian families of IS fighters; And in sport, the AFL sacks the head of its appeals board.

item.title: Australian women and children attempt repatriation from Syrian detention camp
item.description: A group of Australian women and children have left a Syrian refugee camp in a renewed attempt to return home.

item.title: Dawn services sullied by boos
item.description: Australians have marked Anzac Day across the country and in Gallipoli with ceremonies remembering those who have served. Despite the solemnity of the occasion, dawn services in Sydney, Melbourne and Perth have been marred by heckling. Veterans’ groups and political leaders condemned the incidents as a disrespectful violation of Anzac Day.

item.title: Ultra-processed foods damage your focus and may increase dementia risk, study finds
item.description: Even small increases in ultra-processed foods can lead to poorer brain function, regardless of diet quality, a new study has found.

item.title: 'Cruel, immoral': US to allow firing squads, electrocution and gassing in federal executions
item.description: The US is one of very few Western nations that still uses the death penalty, although public support for it has gradually declined.
